text: On 10-29-2018 and 2-6-2019, the patient underwent bronchoscopy.
She was found to have a long right main stem stump that connected with the right pleural space.

She was reluctant to have surgery when she originally presented in October, so she left and went back home in spite of being offered surgical optimal therapy, which would have been to shorten the stump.
We performed the repeat bronchoscopy to acquire evaluation of the right main stem bronchus, which appeared to be too long, and to establish pulmonary hygiene and obtain sculptures.
This illustrates the operation of incision and debridement of the right chest.
The anterior thoracotomy is created and we begin by raising an intercostal muscle flap.
We excise the right ribs on the anterior aspect to create a thoracoscopic window through which we can pass a camera to evaluate the internal contents within the chest.
We use a Madsen periosteor rib elevator to elevate the bone from the chest wall, and then use bovie electrocautery to create the opening for the window.
Surgicel is seen in the wound to the patient's right and the left side of the image, and the incision that was originally used to enter this space is seen in the top left.
We obtained immediate cultures of the space and placed a camera inside the chest.
The white structures that can be seen are branches of the pulmonary artery that are floating in the open space.
The blue structures that are seen are proline that was used to tie off either bronchial stump or pulmonary artery branches.

The purulent pustular material that's being evacuated was first sent for culture and then a suction device is used to take away some of the inside of the purulent material that had been collecting within her chest.
The pulmonary artery is seen just to the right with multiple branches tied off and some branches just open and floating within the pleural space.
We felt that it was not safe to further debride this area without gaining proximal control of the pulmonary artery.

We felt that exploration beyond what we did in this case would be almost impossible to do safely, and so adjacent to the area of the pulmonary artery, but far away from the pulmonary artery, we performed debridement and packing.
You can now see air evacuating from the right main stem bronchial stump as the patient was being ventilated.

It's important to lower the FiO2 while you're exploring the chest and to make sure that you do not use argon plasma coagulation nor do you use bovie electrocautery in these regions so that the patient does not catch on fire.
Having an airway fire in this case would be catastrophic.

We also were very careful about the way that we manipulated the pulmonary artery, and you can see the terminal end of the bronchoscope coming through the tracheobronchial tree as it's seen in the right pleural space.
At this point, we felt that further exploration and debridement would not be safe, and so we electively packed with Dakin soaked Curlex gauze into the right pleural space.
We then performed mobilization of the right-sided pectoralis flap, but left it in situ to mature.
In February 8th, 2019, we shortened the right pulmonary artery stump and the bronchial stump.
And this video will begin to show you how we did that.
We initially performed video bronchoscopy into the left side and placed an armored endotracheal tube with a balloon cuff, closing off the left side.
We had ECMO available should it have been needed, but did not have to use it during the case.
We had the goal of stapling off the right main stem bronchus as is shown in the illustration with the dotted line.

The median sternotomy provided us excellent exposure of the unoperated field with a fresh tissue plane and proximal dissection in a safer manner of the right main stem bronchus as well as the right proximal pulmonary artery.
Trying to do this dissection from the right chest would have been impossible and bleeding was the biggest risk.
Being able to control everything from the right chest is impossible and thus the anterior median sternotomy was the best approach.
A curved clamp is placed around the superior vena cava and an umbilical tape is circumferentially placed for retraction.
The umbilical tape can safely be placed circumferentially around the superior vena cava to provide easy retraction laterally to expose the proximal branch of the pulmonary artery on the right side.
We then began to dissect the trachea.

In circumstances where a bronchopleural fistula is leaking into the right pleural space, it's important not to enter the right pleural space too early as gas emanating into the right pleural space would pose the risk of an airway fire.
We used electrocautery and bovie in this circumstance only before violating or opening the trachea and tried to keep the trachea closed during this entire dissection to prevent airway fire.
This exposure shows you complete dissection of the left main stem bronchus, the right main stem bronchus, and the trachea.
To facilitate healing it is important only to dissect that portion of the trachea that you plan on encircling

mobilizing and stapling, and not to dissect an unnecessary portion of the trachea, as this will sometimes cause necrosis and eliminate good blood supply of the trachea that you definitely need to provide for healing.
The suction device can also provide retraction.
The assistant's left finger is used to push down on the aorta.

If the patient doesn't tolerate near occlusion of the aorta with retraction of the aortic root and the arch, then the patient could be placed on cardiopulmonary bypass for safer decompression of the aorta and exposure.
In this particular case, we did not have to go on cardiopulmonary bypass and we were easily able to compress the aorta and provide adequate exposure.
In certain circumstances, it might be necessary to provide another cannula of the superior and inferior vena cava if the patient has to go on bypass, but this was also not necessary.
In this circumstance, we only had to encircle the right main stem bronchus as well as the right main pulmonary artery, which is being performed in this image.

By encircling the right main pulmonary artery and the right main stem bronchus with umbilical tape, we've now identified both structures that need to be dissected very clearly so that such structures can be encircled, stapled, and divided.
We used vascular load TA staplers that were quite big and bulky in this circumstance, but easily able to pass through and clearly provide good sealing.
This vascular load white load stapler was used to pass around the right main stem pulmonary artery.
The umbilical tape is used to elevate the right main stem pulmonary artery just off of the trunk.

By passing this circumferentially around the right main pulmonary artery, we can pass two staple loads, firing them next to one another for multiple layers of stapling before we transect the distal pulmonary artery.
In this circumstance, after deploying the anvil, it's important to remove the umbilical tape or retract the umbilical tape so that it is clearly out of the way.
In this circumstance, we've now fired but not divided the pulmonary artery.
We've released the anvil and removed the stapler.
In this circumstance, we elected to do this one more time,
so that we could easily achieve the best control of the vascular proximal aspect of the pulmonary artery.
Another curved clamp is passed around this area and used to dissect clearly.
We again place the umbilical tape around the pulmonary artery and pull it back up once again.
The second vascular load TA stapler is now being passed around the proximal aspect of the pulmonary artery and is now fired again after the anvil is deployed and the umbilical tape is removed.
We compress the ascending aorta and this time move the stapler so that it is farther distal away from the truncus portion of the pulmonary artery.
This additional layer of stapling is for safety.

Once we are clearly able to expose the proximal staple line with the PNUT device and place the distal stapler farther out on the pulmonary artery tree towards the distal portion of the right main pulmonary artery, we then deploy the stapler.
We did not transect it as it was not necessary to divide this as no tissue was being removed distally.
We then began to dissect and expose that portion of the pulmonary artery that could be divided to provide better exposure of the tracheobronchial tree.
In some cases, it may not be necessary to divide the pulmonary artery, and in others, it may be helpful to provide better exposure of the right main stem bronchus.
In this circumstance, we've now placed umbilical tape around the right main stem bronchus, and we are using this when needed.
Using an additional fired staple load to go onto the pulmonary artery may or may not be necessary when trying to make sure that we have a good seal.
In some circumstances, making two staple loads on the proximal aspect and one staple load on the distal aspect may be deemed the safest.
In this circumstance, you can see how the stapler is being positioned to divide that pulmonary artery branch farther out.
This is at the point where we decided that we did have to divide the pulmonary artery to provide better exposure of the right main stem bronchus.

In this circumstance, we were able to maneuver to exactly where we wanted on the more proximal and distal aspects of the pulmonary artery so that good healthy staple lines were existing prior to dividing and dissecting the pulmonary artery.
As you can see, we begin to use scissors to divide between those two staple loads, allowing us to completely transect the right main stem pulmonary artery.
We have proximal and distal control.
We do not expect any bleeding at this point.
The patient has not required cardiopulmonary bypass.
The umbilical tape is still left retracting the superior vena cava for exposure.
Once we've completed this, from the surgeon's view, you can see we continue to dissect out the right main stem bronchus.

Using a finger of the assistant on the left side of the patient to decompress the ascending aorta to some degree, we then continue to monitor the pressures as we use a right angle and a bovie electrocautery to dissect around the right main stem bronchus.

Continuing on with this dissection, we can gently dissect out the entire carina, right main stone bronchus, and proximal left main stone bronchus to make sure that we are shoring up the right main stone bronchus as close to the trachea as possible, as seen in the photograph to the left.
Because this is bronchus, we are using a green load endo-GIA stapler on the healthier portion of the bronchus, far away from that margin that was in the right pleural space.
We are encircling and stapling and dividing the right main stem bronchus with the TA load stapler.

In an ultimate setting, if we had enough clearance, we would staple this on both proximal and distal aspects prior to dividing to limit contamination of the mediastinum and infection in this area as this was a great concern.

With all of the purulent material in the right chest, it was important to also evacuate that purulent material and serially pack the right chest prior to taking the patient for the median sternotomy to prevent median sternotomy wound dehiscence.

Of course, it was not made completely sterile, but making every attempt we could to clean out the right chest prior to performing the median sternotomy is a critical portion of this aspect of treatment for this patient.
We then deploy the green load endogia stapler a second time and use a sharp scalpel to divide between the two structures.
Once the scalpel is used to divide between the two staple loads of the tracheobronchial tree of the right main stem bronchus, we are able to clearly see a small opening.
We use scissors to complete this transection and then we'll have to debride and resect and remove the remaining portion of the bronchus from the right side of the chest when we can.
Once the right tracheobronchial tree of the right main stem bronchus has been divided and is stapled flush with the trachea and the left main stem bronchus, the stapler can now be removed.
It is a very difficult angle to film this and we had to place the camera far enough away so that we had clearance for the stapler.
In this circumstance, you can now see the right main stem bronchus has been transected.
It is important to make sure that buttressing this area with additional sutures and a long proline suture can provide clearance.
We make sure that there's no air leak and then begin to close the chest.
Confirming hemostasis, using sternal wires to close the chest, twisting the sternal wires down as is done in the usual routine fashion, and performing a triple layer closure.

We also applied a wound vac to help with evacuating any tissue and infection and drainage and material that might have been in the wound of the median sternotomy until we knew that we had absolutely cleaned out the right pleural space and had good control.
On February 11th, we mobilized the right pectoralis muscle flap and placed it into the chest.
